skill.name: nature-figure
skill.description: Submission-grade Nature/high-impact journal figure workflow for Python or R. Use whenever the user asks to create, revise, audit, or polish manuscript figures, multi-panel scientific plots, figures4papers-style matplotlib plots, or journal-ready SVG/PDF/TIFF outputs, especially for Nature-family or other high-impact journals. Before plotting, define the figure's conclusion, evidence logic, export needs, and review risks. If the user has not chosen Python or R, ask "Python or R?" and stop. Use only the selected backend for figure generation, previewing, exporting, and QA. Supports matplotlib/seaborn and ggplot2/patchwork/ComplexHeatmap. Not for dashboards or Illustrator/Figma-first infographics. Also trigger on general academic-writing figure needs even without the word "Nature", such as making figures/plots for a paper, scientific/academic plotting, data visualization for a manuscript, and Chinese phrasings like 论文配图、学术写作配图、科研绘图、科研作图、画图、作图、出图、论文图表、可视化.
skill.summary: Create publication-quality Nature-style scientific figures with Python or R, following a backend-gated workflow for journal-ready SVG/PDF/TIFF outputs.
skill.author: Community contribution, refactored into static/dynamic layers

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 783 potential issues, but evaluation confirms the vast majority are false positives. The skill is a legitimate scientific figure-generation toolkit for academic publishing. Flagged patterns are explainable: 'C2 keywords' are model names (DCRNN, GTS) in comparison charts, 'weak crypto' triggers are hex color codes, 'os file operations' are figure output directory creation, and 'shell backtick' matches are markdown inline code formatting. No malicious intent or data exfiltration patterns found.

low_findings.title: Markdown inline code patterns
low_findings.description: Numerous markdown files use backtick formatting for code references and shell command examples. These are documentation patterns, not shell execution. All detected 'shell backtick execution' findings in README.md, SKILL.md, and references/ are markdown rendering of code samples, not actual shell invocation.

low_findings.title: Output directory creation in figure scripts
low_findings.description: Python plotting scripts use os.makedirs('./figures/', exist_ok=True) to create output directories before saving figure files. This is standard scientific plotting workflow behavior, not filesystem manipulation.

content.user_title: Create journal-ready scientific figures for Nature submissions
content.value_statement: Researchers struggle to produce submission-quality figures that meet Nature and high-impact journal standards. This skill provides a backend-gated workflow with Python or R, ensuring figures are scientifically clear, aesthetically polished, and export-ready.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of multi-panel scientific figures for Nature and high-impact journals
Supports matplotlib/seaborn for Python and ggplot2/patchwork/ComplexHeatmap for R backends
Enforces a backend-selection gate, requiring explicit Python or R choice before proceeding
Provides archetype-first composition with hero panels, restrained palettes, and clear conclusions
Exports journal-ready SVG, PDF, and TIFF outputs at appropriate DPI
Includes worked examples and a chart-atlas for reference across scientific domains
limitations: Does not support Illustrator or Figma-first infographic workflows
Requires explicit backend selection; will halt and ask rather than auto-choose
Not designed for dashboards or interactive visualizations
Reference material loads only on demand to keep context lean

use_cases.title: Preparing figures for a Nature manuscript submission
use_cases.description: A researcher needs multi-panel figures with journal-compliant formatting, color schemes, and export resolution for Nature-family journal submission.
use_cases.target_user: Academic researchers submitting to Nature or Cell-family journals

use_cases.title: Polishing existing scientific plots for publication
use_cases.description: A PhD student has draft matplotlib or ggplot2 plots and needs them refined to publication standards with proper typography, spacing, and export settings.
use_cases.target_user: Graduate students preparing figures for their first paper

use_cases.title: Creating reproducible figure workflows for a lab
use_cases.description: A lab head wants a consistent, reproducible figure-creation pipeline that lab members can follow with either Python or R, ensuring visual consistency across papers.
use_cases.target_user: Principal investigators establishing lab figure standards

prompt_templates.title: Basic figure creation request
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to create a 3-panel figure for my Nature submission showing gene expression across three conditions. Python or R?
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new figure with backend selection

prompt_templates.title: Figure refinement with existing data
prompt_templates.prompt: Here's my data file and draft matplotlib script. Can you help me refine it to Nature publication standards with proper DPI, font choices, and panel arrangement?

prompt_templates.title: Specific chart type guidance
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to make a complex heatmap with annotations for a Nature paper. My backend is R. Can you guide me through ComplexHeatmap best practices?

prompt_templates.title: QA and export verification
prompt_templates.prompt: I've finished my figure and need to verify it meets submission standards. Can you run through the QA contract and help with final export to PDF and TIFF?

output_examples.input: Create a bar chart comparing three methods across two datasets with error bars
output_examples.output: A 2-panel figure with grouped bars, clear error bars (mean +/- std), Helvetica typography at 24pt, color-coded methods with a legend, exported as both PDF (vector) and PNG (300 DPI) suitable for Nature submission.

output_examples.input: I need a heatmap showing gene expression across 5 cell types with hierarchical clustering
output_examples.output: A ComplexHeatmap visualization with dendrograms on rows and columns, a color scale legend, annotated row and column labels, exported at 300 DPI as both PDF and PNG with publication-ready sizing.

output_examples.input: Polish this scatter plot for a Nature figure
output_examples.output: A refined scatter plot with restrained color palette, clear axis labels at appropriate font size, legend positioned to avoid data occlusion, white background with minimal spines, saved at 300 DPI in vector and raster formats.

best_practices: Always confirm backend (Python or R) before generating any plotting code
Define the figure's core conclusion and evidence chain before writing any plotting script
Use vector formats (PDF/SVG) for figures with text or sharp lines, and raster formats (PNG/TIFF) at 300+ DPI for image-heavy figures
Apply archetype-first composition: identify the hero panel, use restrained palettes, and make statistics an integral part of the figure
anti_patterns: Do not auto-select a backend; always ask the user to choose Python or R explicitly
Do not use more than 5-7 distinct colors in a single figure; Nature style favors restrained palettes
Do not include 3D effects, shadows, or decorative gridlines in publication figures
Do not skip the figure contract step; every figure must have a stated conclusion and evidence logic

faq.question: What backends does this skill support?
faq.answer: Python with matplotlib/seaborn, or R with ggplot2/patchwork/ComplexHeatmap. You must choose one explicitly.

faq.question: Will the skill auto-select Python or R for me?
faq.answer: No. The skill enforces a blocking gate and asks you to choose before generating any code.

faq.question: What output formats are supported?
faq.answer: PDF and SVG for vector graphics, PNG and TIFF at 300 DPI for raster images, matching journal submission requirements.

faq.question: Is this skill suitable for dashboards?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for static publication figures only, not interactive dashboards or web visualizations.

faq.question: Can I use this for non-Nature journals?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow generalizes to Cell, Science, and other high-impact journals with similar quality standards.

faq.question: Does the skill run code or only provide guidance?
faq.answer: The skill provides detailed guidance, code examples, and a QA contract. Code execution depends on your Claude Code environment.
